scenarios.task: We've already spent $2.1M building out this new warehouse automation system over the last year. It's behind schedule and the latest projection shows it'll take another $800K to finish, with uncertain payback. Given how much we've already invested, I don't think we can walk away now — we should finish it.
criteria: Explicitly names this as sunk-cost reasoning and states the $2.1M already spent is irrelevant to the forward decision
Reframes the decision as a comparison between the incremental $800K plus its expected payback versus the next-best use of that same $800K
States that 'we've already invested this much' is not itself evidence the project is worth finishing
Recommends evaluating the remaining spend on its own forward-looking merits, independent of the amount already sunk

scenarios.task: For the board deck, I want to show next year's revenue forecast as a single number — $14.37M — since that looks more rigorous and precise than a range. The underlying assumptions (close rate, average deal size, churn) each have real uncertainty, but a specific number is more decision-useful for the board than a vague range.
criteria: Rejects presenting a single point estimate to that level of precision as false precision, given the stated uncertainty in the underlying assumptions
States that a specific-looking number implies confidence the inputs don't actually support
Recommends presenting a range (best/base/worst) or explicit sensitivity around the 2-3 assumptions the forecast is most sensitive to, instead of laundering uncertainty into one confident figure
Notes that a range is more decision-useful for a board, not less, because it shows what would have to be true for the outcome to differ

scenarios.task: Gross margin improved from 42% to 51% this quarter compared to last — great news, our cost efficiency initiatives are clearly working as planned.
criteria: Checks whether the margin improvement came from the numerator (costs actually falling) or the denominator (revenue growing/mix shifting) before crediting the cost-efficiency initiative
Names this explicitly as a denominator-blindness check — a ratio can move because either side changed
States that a margin percentage improvement is not on its own evidence that the specific initiative caused it
Recommends pulling the underlying cost and revenue figures separately to confirm which side actually moved and by how much

scenarios.task: We're evaluating whether to open a new retail location. The projection shows it'll be profitable — positive net income by year two. Since it makes money, I think that's enough to justify approving it.
criteria: Rejects 'profitable in isolation' as sufficient justification, invoking opportunity cost — the relevant comparison is against the next-best use of the same capital, not just profit vs. no investment
Asks what return the same capital could generate in the next-best alternative (another location, a different channel, paying down debt, returning capital) before accepting the recommendation
States that a project can be profitable and still be a bad decision if a better-returning alternative exists for the same capital
Frames the actual decision as a comparison between alternatives, not a binary profitable/not-profitable test on this one project
